title: Chapter 9
content_en:
After two years I remember the rest of that day, and that night and the next day, only as an endless drill of police and photographers and newspaper men in and out of GatsbyтАЩs front door. A rope stretched across the main gate and a policeman by it kept out the curious, but little boys soon discovered that they could enter through my yard, and there were always a few of them clustered open-mouthed about the pool. Someone with a positive manner, perhaps a detective, used the expression тАЬmadmanтАЭ as he bent over WilsonтАЩs body that afternoon, and the adventitious authority of his voice set the key for the newspaper reports next morning.
Most of those reports were a nightmareтБатАФgrotesque, circumstantial, eager, and untrue. When MichaelisтАЩs testimony at the inquest brought to light WilsonтАЩs suspicions of his wife I thought the whole tale would shortly be served up in racy pasquinadeтБатАФbut Catherine, who might have said anything, didnтАЩt say a word. She showed a surprising amount of character about it tooтБатАФlooked at the coroner with determined eyes under that corrected brow of hers, and swore that her sister had never seen Gatsby, that her sister was completely happy with her husband, that her sister had been into no mischief whatever. She convinced herself of it, and cried into her handkerchief, as if the very suggestion was more than she could endure. So Wilson was reduced to a man тАЬderanged by griefтАЭ in order that the case might remain in its simplest form. And it rested there.
But all this part of it seemed remote and unessential. I found myself on GatsbyтАЩs side, and alone. From the moment I telephoned news of the catastrophe to West Egg village, every surmise about him, and every practical question, was referred to me. At first I was surprised and confused; then, as he lay in his house and didnтАЩt move or breathe or speak, hour upon hour, it grew upon me that I was responsible, because no one else was interestedтБатАФinterested, I mean, with that intense personal interest to which everyone has some vague right at the end.
I called up Daisy half an hour after we found him, called her instinctively and without hesitation. But she and Tom had gone away early that afternoon, and taken baggage with them.
тАЬLeft no address?тАЭ
тАЬNo.тАЭ
тАЬSay when theyтАЩd be back?тАЭ
тАЬNo.тАЭ
тАЬAny idea where they are? How I could reach them?тАЭ
тАЬI donтАЩt know. CanтАЩt say.тАЭ
I wanted to get somebody for him. I wanted to go into the room where he lay and reassure him: тАЬIтАЩll get somebody for you, Gatsby. DonтАЩt worry. Just trust me and IтАЩll get somebody for youтБатАФтАЭ
Meyer WolfshiemтАЩs name wasnтАЩt in the phone book. The butler gave me his office address on Broadway, and I called Information, but by the time I had the number it was long after five, and no one answered the phone.
тАЬWill you ring again?тАЭ
тАЬIтАЩve rung three times.тАЭ
тАЬItтАЩs very important.тАЭ
тАЬSorry. IтАЩm afraid no oneтАЩs there.тАЭ
I went back to the drawing-room and thought for an instant that they were chance visitors, all these official people who suddenly filled it. But, though they drew back the sheet and looked at Gatsby with shocked eyes, his protest continued in my brain:
тАЬLook here, old sport, youтАЩve got to get somebody for me. YouтАЩve got to try hard. I canтАЩt go through this alone.тАЭ
Someone started to ask me questions, but I broke away and going upstairs looked hastily through the unlocked parts of his deskтБатАФheтАЩd never told me definitely that his parents were dead. But there was nothingтБатАФonly the picture of Dan Cody, a token of forgotten violence, staring down from the wall.
Next morning I sent the butler to New York with a letter to Wolfshiem, which asked for information and urged him to come out on the next train. That request seemed superfluous when I wrote it. I was sure heтАЩd start when he saw the newspapers, just as I was sure thereтАЩd be a wire from Daisy before noonтБатАФbut neither a wire nor Mr.┬аWolfshiem arrived; no one arrived except more police and photographers and newspaper men. When the butler brought back WolfshiemтАЩs answer I began to have a feeling of defiance, of scornful solidarity between Gatsby and me against them all.
Dear Mr.┬аCarraway. This has been one of the most terrible shocks of my life to me I hardly can believe it that it is true at all. Such a mad act as that man did should make us all think. I cannot come down now as I am tied up in some very important business and cannot get mixed up in this thing now. If there is anything I can do a little later let me know in a letter by Edgar. I hardly know where I am when I hear about a thing like this and am completely knocked down and out.
Yours truly
Meyer Wolfshiem
and then hasty addenda beneath:
Let me know about the funeral etc do not know his family at all.
When the phone rang that afternoon and Long Distance said Chicago was calling I thought this would be Daisy at last. But the connection came through as a manтАЩs voice, very thin and far away.
тАЬThis is Slagle speakingтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ
тАЬYes?тАЭ The name was unfamiliar.
тАЬHell of a note, isnтАЩt it? Get my wire?тАЭ
тАЬThere havenтАЩt been any wires.тАЭ
тАЬYoung ParkeтАЩs in trouble,тАЭ he said rapidly. тАЬThey picked him up when he handed the bonds over the counter. They got a circular from New York giving тАЩem the numbers just five minutes before. What dтАЩyou know about that, hey? You never can tell in these hick townsтБатАФтАЭ
тАЬHello!тАЭ I interrupted breathlessly. тАЬLook hereтБатАФthis isnтАЩt Mr.┬аGatsby. Mr.┬аGatsbyтАЩs dead.тАЭ
There was a long silence on the other end of the wire, followed by an exclamationтБатАКтБатАж then a quick squawk as the connection was broken.
I think it was on the third day that a telegram signed Henry C. Gatz arrived from a town in Minnesota. It said only that the sender was leaving immediately and to postpone the funeral until he came.
It was GatsbyтАЩs father, a solemn old man, very helpless and dismayed, bundled up in a long cheap ulster against the warm September day. His eyes leaked continuously with excitement, and when I took the bag and umbrella from his hands he began to pull so incessantly at his sparse grey beard that I had difficulty in getting off his coat. He was on the point of collapse, so I took him into the music-room and made him sit down while I sent for something to eat. But he wouldnтАЩt eat, and the glass of milk spilled from his trembling hand.
тАЬI saw it in the Chicago newspaper,тАЭ he said. тАЬIt was all in the Chicago newspaper. I started right away.тАЭ
тАЬI didnтАЩt know how to reach you.тАЭ
His eyes, seeing nothing, moved ceaselessly about the room.
тАЬIt was a madman,тАЭ he said. тАЬHe must have been mad.тАЭ
тАЬWouldnтАЩt you like some coffee?тАЭ I urged him.
тАЬI donтАЩt want anything. IтАЩm all right now, Mr.тБатАФтАЭ
тАЬCarraway.тАЭ
тАЬWell, IтАЩm all right now. Where have they got Jimmy?тАЭ
I took him into the drawing-room, where his son lay, and left him there. Some little boys had come up on the steps and were looking into the hall; when I told them who had arrived, they went reluctantly away.
After a little while Mr.┬аGatz opened the door and came out, his mouth ajar, his face flushed slightly, his eyes leaking isolated and unpunctual tears. He had reached an age where death no longer has the quality of ghastly surprise, and when he looked around him now for the first time and saw the height and splendour of the hall and the great rooms opening out from it into other rooms, his grief began to be mixed with an awed pride. I helped him to a bedroom upstairs; while he took off his coat and vest I told him that all arrangements had been deferred until he came.
тАЬI didnтАЩt know what youтАЩd want, Mr.┬аGatsbyтБатАФтАЭ
тАЬGatz is my name.тАЭ
тАЬтБатАФMr.┬аGatz. I thought you might want to take the body West.тАЭ
He shook his head.
тАЬJimmy always liked it better down East. He rose up to his position in the East. Were you a friend of my boyтАЩs, Mr.тБатАФ?тАЭ
тАЬWe were close friends.тАЭ
тАЬHe had a big future before him, you know. He was only a young man, but he had a lot of brain power here.тАЭ
He touched his head impressively, and I nodded.
тАЬIf heтАЩd of lived, heтАЩd of been a great man. A man like James J. Hill. HeтАЩd of helped build up the country.тАЭ
тАЬThatтАЩs true,тАЭ I said, uncomfortably.
He fumbled at the embroidered coverlet, trying to take it from the bed, and lay down stifflyтБатАФwas instantly asleep.
That night an obviously frightened person called up, and demanded to know who I was before he would give his name.
тАЬThis is Mr.┬аCarraway,тАЭ I said.
тАЬOh!тАЭ He sounded relieved. тАЬThis is Klipspringer.тАЭ
I was relieved too, for that seemed to promise another friend at GatsbyтАЩs grave. I didnтАЩt want it to be in the papers and draw a sightseeing crowd, so IтАЩd been calling up a few people myself. They were hard to find.
тАЬThe funeralтАЩs tomorrow,тАЭ I said. тАЬThree oтАЩclock, here at the house. I wish youтАЩd tell anybody whoтАЩd be interested.тАЭ
тАЬOh, I will,тАЭ he broke out hastily. тАЬOf course IтАЩm not likely to see anybody, but if I do.тАЭ
His tone made me suspicious.
тАЬOf course youтАЩll be there yourself.тАЭ
тАЬWell, IтАЩll certainly try. What I called up about isтБатАФтАЭ
тАЬWait a minute,тАЭ I interrupted. тАЬHow about saying youтАЩll come?тАЭ
тАЬWell, the fact isтБатАФthe truth of the matter is that IтАЩm staying with some people up here in Greenwich, and they rather expect me to be with them tomorrow. In fact, thereтАЩs a sort of picnic or something. Of course IтАЩll do my best to get away.тАЭ
I ejaculated an unrestrained тАЬHuh!тАЭ and he must have heard me, for he went on nervously:
тАЬWhat I called up about was a pair of shoes I left there. I wonder if itтАЩd be too much trouble to have the butler send them on. You see, theyтАЩre tennis shoes, and IтАЩm sort of helpless without them. My address is care of B. F.тБатАФтАЭ
I didnтАЩt hear the rest of the name, because I hung up the receiver.
After that I felt a certain shame for GatsbyтБатАФone gentleman to whom I telephoned implied that he had got what he deserved. However, that was my fault, for he was one of those who used to sneer most bitterly at Gatsby on the courage of GatsbyтАЩs liquor, and I should have known better than to call him.
The morning of the funeral I went up to New York to see Meyer Wolfshiem; I couldnтАЩt seem to reach him any other way. The door that I pushed open, on the advice of an elevator boy, was marked тАЬThe Swastika Holding Company,тАЭ and at first there didnтАЩt seem to be anyone inside. But when IтАЩd shouted тАЬhelloтАЭ several times in vain, an argument broke out behind a partition, and presently a lovely Jewess appeared at an interior door and scrutinized me with black hostile eyes.
тАЬNobodyтАЩs in,тАЭ she said. тАЬMr.┬аWolfshiemтАЩs gone to Chicago.тАЭ
The first part of this was obviously untrue, for someone had begun to whistle тАЬThe Rosary,тАЭ tunelessly, inside.
тАЬPlease say that Mr.┬аCarraway wants to see him.тАЭ
тАЬI canтАЩt get him back from Chicago, can I?тАЭ
At this moment a voice, unmistakably WolfshiemтАЩs, called тАЬStella!тАЭ from the other side of the door.
тАЬLeave your name on the desk,тАЭ she said quickly. тАЬIтАЩll give it to him when he gets back.тАЭ
тАЬBut I know heтАЩs there.тАЭ
She took a step toward me and began to slide her hands indignantly up and down her hips.
тАЬYou young men think you can force your way in here any time,тАЭ she scolded. тАЬWeтАЩre getting sickantired of it. When I say heтАЩs in Chicago, heтАЩs in Chicago.тАЭ
I mentioned Gatsby.
тАЬOh-h!тАЭ She looked at me over again. тАЬWill you justтБатАФWhat was your name?тАЭ
She vanished. In a moment Meyer Wolfshiem stood solemnly in the doorway, holding out both hands. He drew me into his office, remarking in a reverent voice that it was a sad time for all of us, and offered me a cigar.
тАЬMy memory goes back to when first I met him,тАЭ he said. тАЬA young major just out of the army and covered over with medals he got in the war. He was so hard up he had to keep on wearing his uniform because he couldnтАЩt buy some regular clothes. First time I saw him was when he came into WinebrennerтАЩs poolroom at Forty-third Street and asked for a job. He hadnтАЩt eat anything for a couple of days. тАШCome on have some lunch with me,тАЩ I said. He ate more than four dollarsтАЩ worth of food in half an hour.тАЭ
тАЬDid you start him in business?тАЭ I inquired.
тАЬStart him! I made him.тАЭ
тАЬOh.тАЭ
тАЬI raised him up out of nothing, right out of the gutter. I saw right away he was a fine-appearing, gentlemanly young man, and when he told me he was at Oggsford I knew I could use him good. I got him to join the American Legion and he used to stand high there. Right off he did some work for a client of mine up to Albany. We were so thick like that in everythingтАЭтБатАФhe held up two bulbous fingersтБатАФтАЬalways together.тАЭ
I wondered if this partnership had included the WorldтАЩs Series transaction in 1919.
тАЬNow heтАЩs dead,тАЭ I said after a moment. тАЬYou were his closest friend, so I know youтАЩll want to come to his funeral this afternoon.тАЭ
тАЬIтАЩd like to come.тАЭ
тАЬWell, come then.тАЭ
The hair in his nostrils quivered slightly, and as he shook his head his eyes filled with tears.
тАЬI canтАЩt do itтБатАФI canтАЩt get mixed up in it,тАЭ he said.
тАЬThereтАЩs nothing to get mixed up in. ItтАЩs all over now.тАЭ
тАЬWhen a man gets killed I never like to get mixed up in it in any way. I keep out. When I was a young man it was differentтБатАФif a friend of mine died, no matter how, I stuck with them to the end. You may think thatтАЩs sentimental, but I mean itтБатАФto the bitter end.тАЭ
I saw that for some reason of his own he was determined not to come, so I stood up.
тАЬAre you a college man?тАЭ he inquired suddenly.
For a moment I thought he was going to suggest a тАЬgonnegtion,тАЭ but he only nodded and shook my hand.
тАЬLet us learn to show our friendship for a man when he is alive and not after he is dead,тАЭ he suggested. тАЬAfter that my own rule is to let everything alone.тАЭ
When I left his office the sky had turned dark and I got back to West Egg in a drizzle. After changing my clothes I went next door and found Mr.┬аGatz walking up and down excitedly in the hall. His pride in his son and in his sonтАЩs possessions was continually increasing and now he had something to show me.
тАЬJimmy sent me this picture.тАЭ He took out his wallet with trembling fingers. тАЬLook there.тАЭ
It was a photograph of the house, cracked in the corners and dirty with many hands. He pointed out every detail to me eagerly. тАЬLook there!тАЭ and then sought admiration from my eyes. He had shown it so often that I think it was more real to him now than the house itself.
тАЬJimmy sent it to me. I think itтАЩs a very pretty picture. It shows up well.тАЭ
тАЬVery well. Had you seen him lately?тАЭ
тАЬHe come out to see me two years ago and bought me the house I live in now. Of course we was broke up when he run off from home, but I see now there was a reason for it. He knew he had a big future in front of him. And ever since he made a success he was very generous with me.тАЭ
He seemed reluctant to put away the picture, held it for another minute, lingeringly, before my eyes. Then he returned the wallet and pulled from his pocket a ragged old copy of a book called Hopalong Cassidy.
тАЬLook here, this is a book he had when he was a boy. It just shows you.тАЭ
He opened it at the back cover and turned it around for me to see. On the last flyleaf was printed the word schedule, and the date September 12, 1906. And underneath:
General Resolves
-
No wasting time at Shafters or [a name, indecipherable]
-
No more smokeing or chewing.
-
Bath every other day
-
Read one improving book or magazine per week
-
Save $5.00 [crossed out] $3.00 per week
-
Be better to parents
No wasting time at Shafters or [a name, indecipherable]
No more smokeing or chewing.
Bath every other day
Read one improving book or magazine per week
Save $5.00 [crossed out] $3.00 per week
Be better to parents
тАЬI came across this book by accident,тАЭ said the old man. тАЬIt just shows you, donтАЩt it?тАЭ
тАЬIt just shows you.тАЭ
тАЬJimmy was bound to get ahead. He always had some resolves like this or something. Do you notice what heтАЩs got about improving his mind? He was always great for that. He told me I et like a hog once, and I beat him for it.тАЭ
He was reluctant to close the book, reading each item aloud and then looking eagerly at me. I think he rather expected me to copy down the list for my own use.
A little before three the Lutheran minister arrived from Flushing, and I began to look involuntarily out the windows for other cars. So did GatsbyтАЩs father. And as the time passed and the servants came in and stood waiting in the hall, his eyes began to blink anxiously, and he spoke of the rain in a worried, uncertain way. The minister glanced several times at his watch, so I took him aside and asked him to wait for half an hour. But it wasnтАЩt any use. Nobody came.
About five oтАЩclock our procession of three cars reached the cemetery and stopped in a thick drizzle beside the gateтБатАФfirst a motor hearse, horribly black and wet, then Mr.┬аGatz and the minister and me in the limousine, and a little later four or five servants and the postman from West Egg, in GatsbyтАЩs station wagon, all wet to the skin. As we started through the gate into the cemetery I heard a car stop and then the sound of someone splashing after us over the soggy ground. I looked around. It was the man with owl-eyed glasses whom I had found marvelling over GatsbyтАЩs books in the library one night three months before.
IтАЩd never seen him since then. I donтАЩt know how he knew about the funeral, or even his name. The rain poured down his thick glasses, and he took them off and wiped them to see the protecting canvas unrolled from GatsbyтАЩs grave.
I tried to think about Gatsby then for a moment, but he was already too far away, and I could only remember, without resentment, that Daisy hadnтАЩt sent a message or a flower. Dimly I heard someone murmur тАЬBlessed are the dead that the rain falls on,тАЭ and then the owl-eyed man said тАЬAmen to that,тАЭ in a brave voice.
We straggled down quickly through the rain to the cars. Owl-eyes spoke to me by the gate.
тАЬI couldnтАЩt get to the house,тАЭ he remarked.
тАЬNeither could anybody else.тАЭ
тАЬGo on!тАЭ He started. тАЬWhy, my God! they used to go there by the hundreds.тАЭ
He took off his glasses and wiped them again, outside and in.
тАЬThe poor son-of-a-bitch,тАЭ he said.
One of my most vivid memories is of coming back West from prep school and later from college at Christmas time. Those who went farther than Chicago would gather in the old dim Union Station at six oтАЩclock of a December evening, with a few Chicago friends, already caught up into their own holiday gaieties, to bid them a hasty goodbye. I remember the fur coats of the girls returning from Miss This-or-ThatтАЩs and the chatter of frozen breath and the hands waving overhead as we caught sight of old acquaintances, and the matchings of invitations: тАЬAre you going to the OrdwaysтАЩ? the HerseysтАЩ? the SchultzesтАЩ?тАЭ and the long green tickets clasped tight in our gloved hands. And last the murky yellow cars of the Chicago, Milwaukee and St.┬аPaul railroad looking cheerful as Christmas itself on the tracks beside the gate.
When we pulled out into the winter night and the real snow, our snow, began to stretch out beside us and twinkle against the windows, and the dim lights of small Wisconsin stations moved by, a sharp wild brace came suddenly into the air. We drew in deep breaths of it as we walked back from dinner through the cold vestibules, unutterably aware of our identity with this country for one strange hour, before we melted indistinguishably into it again.
ThatтАЩs my Middle WestтБатАФnot the wheat or the prairies or the lost Swede towns, but the thrilling returning trains of my youth, and the street lamps and sleigh bells in the frosty dark and the shadows of holly wreaths thrown by lighted windows on the snow. I am part of that, a little solemn with the feel of those long winters, a little complacent from growing up in the Carraway house in a city where dwellings are still called through decades by a familyтАЩs name. I see now that this has been a story of the West, after allтБатАФTom and Gatsby, Daisy and Jordan and I, were all Westerners, and perhaps we possessed some deficiency in common which made us subtly unadaptable to Eastern life.
Even when the East excited me most, even when I was most keenly aware of its superiority to the bored, sprawling, swollen towns beyond the Ohio, with their interminable inquisitions which spared only the children and the very oldтБатАФeven then it had always for me a quality of distortion. West Egg, especially, still figures in my more fantastic dreams. I see it as a night scene by El Greco: a hundred houses, at once conventional and grotesque, crouching under a sullen, overhanging sky and a lustreless moon. In the foreground four solemn men in dress suits are walking along the sidewalk with a stretcher on which lies a drunken woman in a white evening dress. Her hand, which dangles over the side, sparkles cold with jewels. Gravely the men turn in at a houseтБатАФthe wrong house. But no one knows the womanтАЩs name, and no one cares.
After GatsbyтАЩs death the East was haunted for me like that, distorted beyond my eyesтАЩ power of correction. So when the blue smoke of brittle leaves was in the air and the wind blew the wet laundry stiff on the line I decided to come back home.
There was one thing to be done before I left, an awkward, unpleasant thing that perhaps had better have been let alone. But I wanted to leave things in order and not just trust that obliging and indifferent sea to sweep my refuse away. I saw Jordan Baker and talked over and around what had happened to us together, and what had happened afterward to me, and she lay perfectly still, listening, in a big chair.
She was dressed to play golf, and I remember thinking she looked like a good illustration, her chin raised a little jauntily, her hair the colour of an autumn leaf, her face the same brown tint as the fingerless glove on her knee. When I had finished she told me without comment that she was engaged to another man. I doubted that, though there were several she could have married at a nod of her head, but I pretended to be surprised. For just a minute I wondered if I wasnтАЩt making a mistake, then I thought it all over again quickly and got up to say goodbye.
тАЬNevertheless you did throw me over,тАЭ said Jordan suddenly. тАЬYou threw me over on the telephone. I donтАЩt give a damn about you now, but it was a new experience for me, and I felt a little dizzy for a while.тАЭ
We shook hands.
тАЬOh, and do you rememberтАЭтБатАФshe addedтБатАФтАЬa conversation we had once about driving a car?тАЭ
тАЬWhyтБатАФnot exactly.тАЭ
тАЬYou said a bad driver was only safe until she met another bad driver? Well, I met another bad driver, didnтАЩt I? I mean it was careless of me to make such a wrong guess. I thought you were rather an honest, straightforward person. I thought it was your secret pride.тАЭ
тАЬIтАЩm thirty,тАЭ I said. тАЬIтАЩm five years too old to lie to myself and call it honour.тАЭ
She didnтАЩt answer. Angry, and half in love with her, and tremendously sorry, I turned away.
One afternoon late in October I saw Tom Buchanan. He was walking ahead of me along Fifth Avenue in his alert, aggressive way, his hands out a little from his body as if to fight off interference, his head moving sharply here and there, adapting itself to his restless eyes. Just as I slowed up to avoid overtaking him he stopped and began frowning into the windows of a jewellery store. Suddenly he saw me and walked back, holding out his hand.
тАЬWhatтАЩs the matter, Nick? Do you object to shaking hands with me?тАЭ
тАЬYes. You know what I think of you.тАЭ
тАЬYouтАЩre crazy, Nick,тАЭ he said quickly. тАЬCrazy as hell. I donтАЩt know whatтАЩs the matter with you.тАЭ
тАЬTom,тАЭ I inquired, тАЬwhat did you say to Wilson that afternoon?тАЭ
He stared at me without a word, and I knew I had guessed right about those missing hours. I started to turn away, but he took a step after me and grabbed my arm.
тАЬI told him the truth,тАЭ he said. тАЬHe came to the door while we were getting ready to leave, and when I sent down word that we werenтАЩt in he tried to force his way upstairs. He was crazy enough to kill me if I hadnтАЩt told him who owned the car. His hand was on a revolver in his pocket every minute he was in the houseтБатАФтАЭ He broke off defiantly. тАЬWhat if I did tell him? That fellow had it coming to him. He threw dust into your eyes just like he did in DaisyтАЩs, but he was a tough one. He ran over Myrtle like youтАЩd run over a dog and never even stopped his car.тАЭ
There was nothing I could say, except the one unutterable fact that it wasnтАЩt true.
тАЬAnd if you think I didnтАЩt have my share of sufferingтБатАФlook here, when I went to give up that flat and saw that damn box of dog biscuits sitting there on the sideboard, I sat down and cried like a baby. By God it was awfulтБатАФтАЭ
I couldnтАЩt forgive him or like him, but I saw that what he had done was, to him, entirely justified. It was all very careless and confused. They were careless people, Tom and DaisyтБатАФthey smashed up things and creatures and then retreated back into their money or their vast carelessness, or whatever it was that kept them together, and let other people clean up the mess they had madeтБатАКтБатАж
I shook hands with him; it seemed silly not to, for I felt suddenly as though I were talking to a child. Then he went into the jewellery store to buy a pearl necklaceтБатАФor perhaps only a pair of cuff buttonsтБатАФrid of my provincial squeamishness forever.
GatsbyтАЩs house was still empty when I leftтБатАФthe grass on his lawn had grown as long as mine. One of the taxi drivers in the village never took a fare past the entrance gate without stopping for a minute and pointing inside; perhaps it was he who drove Daisy and Gatsby over to East Egg the night of the accident, and perhaps he had made a story about it all his own. I didnтАЩt want to hear it and I avoided him when I got off the train.
I spent my Saturday nights in New York because those gleaming, dazzling parties of his were with me so vividly that I could still hear the music and the laughter, faint and incessant, from his garden, and the cars going up and down his drive. One night I did hear a material car there, and saw its lights stop at his front steps. But I didnтАЩt investigate. Probably it was some final guest who had been away at the ends of the earth and didnтАЩt know that the party was over.
On the last night, with my trunk packed and my car sold to the grocer, I went over and looked at that huge incoherent failure of a house once more. On the white steps an obscene word, scrawled by some boy with a piece of brick, stood out clearly in the moonlight, and I erased it, drawing my shoe raspingly along the stone. Then I wandered down to the beach and sprawled out on the sand.
Most of the big shore places were closed now and there were hardly any lights except the shadowy, moving glow of a ferryboat across the Sound. And as the moon rose higher the inessential houses began to melt away until gradually I became aware of the old island here that flowered once for Dutch sailorsтАЩ eyesтБатАФa fresh, green breast of the new world. Its vanished trees, the trees that had made way for GatsbyтАЩs house, had once pandered in whispers to the last and greatest of all human dreams; for a transitory enchanted moment man must have held his breath in the presence of this continent, compelled into an aesthetic contemplation he neither understood nor desired, face to face for the last time in history with something commensurate to his capacity for wonder.
And as I sat there brooding on the old, unknown world, I thought of GatsbyтАЩs wonder when he first picked out the green light at the end of DaisyтАЩs dock. He had come a long way to this blue lawn, and his dream must have seemed so close that he could hardly fail to grasp it. He did not know that it was already behind him, somewhere back in that vast obscurity beyond the city, where the dark fields of the republic rolled on under the night.
Gatsby believed in the green light, the orgiastic future that year by year recedes before us. It eluded us then, but thatтАЩs no matterтБатАФtomorrow we will run faster, stretch out our arms furtherтБатАКтБатАж And one fine morningтБатАФ
So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past.
